questions.stem: A client with diabetic ketoacidosis is lethargic, has dry mucous membranes, blood pressure 88/54 mmHg, and blood glucose 620 mg/dL. Which intervention should the nurse anticipate first?

options.text: Rapid isotonic fluid replacement.
Long-acting insulin by subcutaneous injection.
A potassium-restricted diet.
Sodium bicarbonate for all clients with DKA.

questions.rationale: The immediate threat in DKA is severe volume depletion and impaired perfusion. Isotonic fluid replacement is the first major intervention, followed by insulin and electrolyte management based on potassium status and response. Subcutaneous long-acting insulin and routine bicarbonate do not address the acute circulatory problem first.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave unstable glucose physiology untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Rapid isotonic fluid replacement" fits the safer response to the immediate threat in DKA is severe volume depletion and impaired perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: Because blood glucose 620 mg/dL suggests unstable glucose physiology, "A potassium-restricted diet" would not correct the active problem. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: Because blood glucose 620 mg/dL suggests unstable glucose physiology, "Sodium bicarbonate for all clients with DKA" would not correct the active problem. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.

questions.takeaway: In DKA with hypotension and dehydration, restore circulating volume first.

questions.stem: A client using a fentanyl patch develops a fever of 102 F (38.9 C). Which instruction should the nurse provide?

options.text: Report the fever promptly because heat can increase fentanyl absorption.
Place a heating pad over the patch to improve comfort.
Cut the patch in half if the client feels too sleepy.
Apply an extra patch if the pain feels worse.

questions.rationale: Heat and fever can increase fentanyl absorption from a transdermal patch and raise the risk of oversedation or respiratory depression. The client should report this promptly and avoid external heat sources. Cutting or doubling patches is unsafe.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. "Report the fever promptly because heat can increase fentanyl absorption" fits the safer response to a client using a fentanyl patch develops a fever of 102 F (38.
distractorRationales.c: "Cut the patch in half if the client feels too sleepy" normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. The cue "A client using a fentanyl patch develops a fever of 102 F (38" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: "Apply an extra patch if the pain feels worse" misses the bedside risk because heat and fever can increase fentanyl absorption from a transdermal patch and raise the risk of oversedation points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: Transdermal fentanyl becomes riskier with fever or heat exposure because absorption can increase.

questions.stem: A client hospitalized with acute pancreatitis develops numbness around the mouth and carpal spasms. Which laboratory value does the nurse anticipate is most likely abnormal?

options.text: Low serum calcium.
High serum sodium.
Low hematocrit.
Low chloride.

questions.rationale: Perioral numbness and carpal spasm suggest neuromuscular irritability from hypocalcemia. Acute pancreatitis can lower serum calcium as part of the disease process. Recognizing the pattern helps the nurse connect clinical signs with the expected electrolyte disturbance.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave neurologic deterioration untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Low serum calcium" fits the safer response to recognizing the pattern helps the nurse connect clinical signs with the expected electrolyte disturbance.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave neurologic deterioration untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Low serum calcium" fits the safer response to recognizing the pattern helps the nurse connect clinical signs with the expected electrolyte disturbance.
distractorRationales.d: "Low chloride" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Acute pancreatitis can lower serum calcium as part of the disease process" raises concern for neurologic deterioration, so neurologic safety assessment is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: Tetany-type symptoms in pancreatitis point toward hypocalcemia.

questions.stem: A client performing peritoneal dialysis reports cloudy outflow and abdominal pain. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Notify the provider because the findings suggest peritonitis.
Tell the client cloudy drainage is expected after dialysis.
Instruct the client to skip the next exchange.
Have the client drink extra water and wait until tomorrow.

questions.rationale: Cloudy dialysate and abdominal pain are classic findings of peritoneal dialysis-associated peritonitis. The nurse should escalate promptly for assessment and treatment. Reassurance or delay can worsen infection and threaten the dialysis access pathway.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave infection progression untreated because it normalizes a finding that needs reassessment. "Notify the provider because the findings suggest peritonitis" fits the safer response to cloudy dialysate and abdominal pain are classic findings of peritoneal dialysis-associated peritonitis.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward premature teaching instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "Cloudy dialysate and abdominal pain are classic findings of peritoneal dialysis-associated peritonitis" makes infection progression the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave infection progression untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Notify the provider because the findings suggest peritonitis" fits the safer response to a client performing peritoneal dialysis reports cloudy outflow and abdominal pain.

questions.takeaway: Cloudy peritoneal dialysis outflow should be treated as peritonitis until proven otherwise.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A pregnant client at 32 weeks reports painless bright red vaginal bleeding. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Apply an external fetal monitor and avoid a vaginal examination.
Perform a sterile vaginal exam to assess dilation.
Instruct the client to walk in the hallway to check if the bleeding slows.
Prepare the client for a routine discharge if contractions are absent.

questions.rationale: Painless bright red bleeding in the third trimester suggests placenta previa. The nurse should assess maternal-fetal status while avoiding vaginal examination because digital exams can worsen hemorrhage when the placenta overlies the cervical os. Early recognition and protection from avoidable bleeding are the priority.

distractorRationales.b: Because the nurse should assess maternal-fetal status while avoiding vaginal examination because digital exams can worsen hemorrhage when the suggests bleeding or anticoagulation injury, "Perform a sterile vaginal exam to assess dilation" would not correct the active problem. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.
distractorRationales.c: "Instruct the client to walk in the hallway to check if the bleeding slows" moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. The cue "The nurse should assess maternal-fetal status while avoiding vaginal examination because digital exams can worsen hemorrhage when the" raises concern for bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so bleeding-risk intervention is needed before ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching.
distractorRationales.d: "Prepare the client for a routine discharge if contractions are absent" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "Early recognition and protection from avoidable bleeding are the priority" raises concern for bleeding or anticoagulation injury, so bleeding-risk intervention is needed before routine observation.

questions.takeaway: Suspected placenta previa means monitor and protect; do not perform a vaginal exam.

questions.stem: A client 3 days after delivery calls the postpartum unit reporting a severe headache, visual spots, and swelling of the hands. Which instruction should the nurse give first?

options.text: Return to the hospital for immediate evaluation.
Take an over-the-counter pain reliever and rest in a dark room.
Limit fluids for the rest of the day.
Wait until the routine postpartum visit next week.

questions.rationale: Severe headache, visual changes, and edema after delivery raise concern for postpartum preeclampsia, which can progress to seizure or stroke. The client needs urgent evaluation rather than home symptom management. Delayed care can miss a serious hypertensive emergency.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave neurologic deterioration untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Return to the hospital for immediate evaluation" fits the safer response to severe headache, visual changes.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of neurologic safety assessment. The stem cue "Severe headache, visual changes" makes neurologic deterioration the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of neurologic safety assessment. The stem cue "Severe headache and visual changes after delivery should be treated as postpartum preeclampsia until proven otherwise" makes neurologic deterioration the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Severe headache and visual changes after delivery should be treated as postpartum preeclampsia until proven otherwise.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client is 4 hours postoperative after a thyroidectomy and develops inspiratory stridor. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Prepare for emergency airway support and notify the provider immediately.
Offer ice chips to soothe the throat.
Document the finding as expected postoperative hoarseness.
Ask the client to speak loudly so the nurse can assess voice quality.

questions.rationale: Stridor after thyroid surgery may indicate airway obstruction from swelling, hematoma, or laryngeal nerve complications and requires immediate action. The nurse should treat it as an airway emergency rather than a routine sore-throat complaint. Delaying airway support can quickly become life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "Delaying airway support can quickly become life-threatening" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward false reassurance instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "Delaying airway support can quickly become life-threatening" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "Delaying airway support can quickly become life-threatening" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Post-thyroidectomy stridor is an airway emergency, not routine hoarseness.

questions.stem: Which statement by a patient taking warfarin shows correct understanding?

options.text: "I will report unusual bleeding or bruising right away."
"I can double the dose if I miss one dose."
"I should stop the medication once I feel better."
"A green salad every day is unsafe no matter what."

questions.rationale: Warfarin can increase bleeding risk, so the patient should know to watch for and report bleeding signs. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Doubling a missed dose can be dangerous. - C Anticoagulants are not stopped casually. - D Vitamin K intake should be consistent, not completely avoided without guidance.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury; ""I can double the dose if I miss one dose."" moves care toward routine care before bleeding-risk intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury; ""I should stop the medication once I feel better."" moves care toward routine care before bleeding-risk intervention.
distractorRationales.d: Because **Why the others are wrong:** - B Doubling a missed dose can be dangerous suggests bleeding or anticoagulation injury, ""A green salad every day is unsafe no matter what."" would not correct the active problem. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.

questions.takeaway: Anticoagulant teaching should focus on bleeding safety and consistency.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: After a patient falls while attempting to ambulate unassisted, the nurse completes an incident report. Which statement about incident reports is correct?

options.text: The incident report should be referenced in the patient's medical record to ensure continuity of care
The incident report is an internal quality improvement tool; it is not referenced in the medical record and is not discoverable in litigation
Incident reports are part of the permanent medical record and must be provided to patients upon request
The nurse should document 'incident report filed' in the nursing notes to alert other providers

questions.rationale: Incident reports (occurrence reports) are internal quality improvement documents: they are NOT referenced in the patient's medical record. Referencing them in the chart (A, D) makes them discoverable in litigation, eliminating their protection as internal QI documents. The nurse documents the clinical facts in the medical record (what happened, patient response, assessment findings, interventions) without mentioning the incident report. In many states, incident reports prepared in anticipation of litigation may be protected from discovery, but only if not referenced in the chart. They are not part of the permanent medical record (C).

distractorRationales.a: "The incident report should be referenced in the patient's medical record to ensure continuity of care" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "The nurse documents the clinical facts in the medical record (what happened, patient response, assessment findings, interventions) without" raises concern for clinical safety risk, so bedside safety action is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: "Incident reports are part of the permanent medical record and must be provided to patients upon request" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "The nurse documents the clinical facts in the medical record (what happened, patient response, assessment findings, interventions) without" raises concern for clinical safety risk, so bedside safety action is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "The nurse should document 'incident report filed' in the nursing notes to alert other providers" moves care toward waiting for a callback before bedside safety action.

questions.takeaway: Incident reports: do NOT reference in the medical record; document clinical facts of the event in the chart only — referencing the incident report makes it legally discoverable.

questions.stem: A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision, and seeing halos around lights. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation.
This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue.
The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home.
These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well.

questions.rationale: Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency that can threaten vision. The nurse should recognize the need for urgent ophthalmic evaluation rather than dismissing the symptoms as routine strain. Time-sensitive treatment matters for outcome.

distractorRationales.b: "This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue" misses the bedside risk because eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma points to clinical safety risk. Nursing safety depends on matching the response to the active clinical risk before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward premature activity instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma" makes clinical safety risk the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency" makes clinical safety risk the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma.

questions.stem: A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg, and sodium 128 mEq/L. Which provider prescription should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids.
Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight.
Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn.
Teach the client about low-sodium foods.

questions.rationale: Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting, and weakness in a client with adrenal insufficiency suggest adrenal crisis. The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. Delaying treatment for further testing can be life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: Because the priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse suggests renal injury, "Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight" would not correct the active problem. Renal protection depends on hydration, medication review, and creatinine follow-up before assuming the study is low risk.
distractorRationales.c: "Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "sodium 128 mEq/L" raises concern for renal injury, so renal protection is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward premature teaching instead of renal protection. The stem cue "A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg" makes renal injury the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Suspected adrenal crisis requires steroids and isotonic fluids right away.

questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Obtain a full allergy history before intervening" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine" fits the safer response to a client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives.
distractorRationales.d: Because the causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "Document the event and continue monitoring" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.
